critique.summary: The article describes the author's process of building a simple Unix shell called 'andsh' in C, covering features like command execution, environment variable expansion, piping, and basic line editing with history and tab completion. It provides code snippets and explanations of key shell concepts such as process forking, execvp, and pipe management.
mainPoints: The shell is implemented as a REPL that reads user input, tokenizes it, expands environment variables, and executes commands.
Commands are run in child processes created by fork, using execvp to replace the child process image, while the parent waits for completion.
Built-in commands like 'cd' must run in the shell process itself to affect the shell's state.
Environment variable expansion is supported for simple cases like $HOME and $? but lacks full shell quoting or complex syntax.
Pipes are implemented using the pipe system call and dup2 to connect the stdout of one process to the stdin of the next.
Line editing, command history, and tab completion are added using the readline library.
The shell is intentionally minimal and incomplete, lacking features like quoting, redirection, and comprehensive built-in commands.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes the implementation details and concepts involved in building a simple Unix shell in C. The use of fork, execvp, waitpid, pipe, and dup2 aligns with standard Unix shell programming practices. The explanations about why 'cd' must be a builtin and how pipes work are correct. The limitations and missing features are honestly acknowledged. The code snippets and descriptions match typical shell implementation approaches, and no factual inaccuracies were found.
considerations: The shell lacks support for quoting, redirection, and complex shell syntax, which are essential for a fully functional shell.
Security considerations such as input sanitization and handling of malicious commands are not discussed.
Performance implications of the readline-based completion and history are briefly mentioned but not deeply analyzed.
The article does not compare this shell implementation to existing shells or discuss compatibility issues.
Error handling and edge cases in command parsing and execution are not fully covered.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the challenges and learning experiences of building a shell, highlighting the complexity of string parsing, job control, and process management. Contributors share personal projects, resources, and insights on the intricacies of shell implementation and Unix process handling.

comments.author: wei03288
comments.text: The pipe section is the part that changes how you think about processes. Once you've manually done the dup2 dance — close write-end in parent, close read-end in child, wire them up — it stops being magic and starts being obvious why `grep | sort | uniq` works at all. The thing that surprised me building a similar toy was how late in the process job control has to come: you can get a working pipe chain surprisingly fast, and then job control (SIGTSTP, tcsetpgrp, the whole mess) costs 5x more than everything else combined.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately describes the technical details of pipe implementation and job control in Unix shells, reflecting deep understanding and practical experience.

comments.author: rrampage
comments.text:
Fun read! I built a minimal Linux shell [0] in c and Zig last year which does not depend on libc. It was a great way to learn about execve, the new-ish clone3 syscall and how Linux starts a process. Parsing strings is the least fun part of the building the shell.

[0] https://gist.github.com/rrampage/5046b60ca2d040bcffb49ee38e8...
comments.analysis: Provides a credible account of shell building with relevant technical details, emphasizing learning through system calls and process creation.

comments.author: lvales
comments.text:
Building a shell is a great exercise, but honestly having to deal with string parsing is such a bother that it robs like 2/3 of the joy along the way. I once built a very simple one in Go [0] as a learning exercise and I stopped once I started getting frustrated with all the corner cases.

[0] https://github.com/lourencovales/codecrafters/blob/master/sh...
comments.analysis: Offers a realistic perspective on the frustrations of string parsing in shell development, which aligns with common developer experiences.

comments.author: hexer303
comments.text: Unix shells are conceptually simple but hide a surprising amount of complexity under the hood that we take for granted. I recently had build my own PTY controller. There were so many edge-cases to deal with. It took weeks of stress testing and writing many tests to get it right.
comments.analysis: Highlights the hidden complexities in shell and PTY implementation, supported by personal experience with testing and edge cases.

comments.author: rigorclaw
comments.text: The pipe implementation section is really clean. Working through fork/exec/dup2 by hand like this is one of those exercises that makes you appreciate how much composability Unix got right. Processes that know nothing about each other just work together because they read stdin and write stdout. I built something similar years ago and the moment pipes actually worked felt like unlocking a cheat code.
comments.analysis: Accurately praises the educational value of implementing pipes and process control, reflecting common understanding among Unix developers.

comments.author: emersion
comments.text: Some time ago I've written an article about a particular aspect of shells, job control: https://emersion.fr/blog/2019/job-control/
comments.analysis: Provides a relevant resource on job control, contributing useful information to the discussion.

comments.author: mzs
comments.text:
Had an assignment to build a shell in a week, how hard could it be?

  controlling terminal
  session leader
  job control
The parser was easy in comparison.
comments.analysis: Concise reflection on the relative difficulty of shell components, accurately noting that job control and terminal management are challenging.

comments.author: ratzkewatzke
comments.text: There's a very good exercise on Codecrafters (https://app.codecrafters.io/courses/shell/overview) to walk you through writing your own shell. I found it enlightening, as well as a good way to learn a new language.
comments.analysis: Recommends a credible learning resource, adding practical value to the discussion.

comments.author: lasgawe
comments.text: Great article. There are many things every developer should do when starting to learn programming or when trying to improve their skills. This is one of them. I once built a shell-like programming language (not an interpreter). If anyone reading this wants to improve their skills, I strongly suggest building your own shell from scratch.
comments.analysis: Encouraging but general comment praising the article without technical depth.

comments.author: doe88
comments.text: Is there a (real) shell whose code is relatively short and self contained and would be valuable to read? This was always something I wanted to do but never quite spent time to look for a good one to explore.
comments.analysis: A reasonable question seeking recommendations, though it does not add technical insight.

comments.author: dirk94018
comments.text: Interesting. I wanted to do toast | bash to let the AI drive the computer but the bash shell really got in the way. Too much complexity. The things that annoy humans, $ expansion, special characters, etc don't work for AI either. Ended up writing a custom shell for AI (and humans). When a tool gets in the way, sometimes it just time to change the tool.
comments.analysis: Shares a personal project and critique of bash complexity, which is a valid perspective though somewhat anecdotal.

comments.author: austy69
comments.text: Fun read. Wonder if you are able to edit text in the shell, or if you need to implement a gap buffer to allow it?
comments.analysis: Poses a relevant technical question about shell input editing, contributing to the discussion.

comments.author: lioeters
comments.text: Link was previously posted by author: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47398749 There are other good quality articles on their site, and maybe deserves the imaginary points.
comments.analysis: Adds no new information or insight, merely pointing out a repeated link.

comments.author: zokier
comments.text: Bit of pedantry but I don't think traditional unix shell (like this) follows repl model; the shell is not usually doing printing of the result of evaluation. Instead the printing happens more as a side effect of the commands.
comments.analysis: Provides a nuanced clarification about shell behavior, which is accurate but somewhat pedantic.
